topic: Economic Objectives and the Role of Government
subject: economics

items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.

items.q: Changes to interest rates in the UK are decided by:
items.correct: MPC of the Bank of England
items.why: It's MPC of the Bank of England. “HM Treasury” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a fiscal policy tool?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Income tax. “Interest rates” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many members does the Bank of England MPC have?
items.why: Exact answer: 9 members. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
